tagline: No rules. No prompts. Just: build me a bracket.

round_of_64.reasoning: Florida is the defending champion with the nation's best adjusted efficiency margin (+30.9). Prairie View A&M is 18-17 from the SWAC with a -16.2 AdjEM. The widest talent gap in the bracket -- no realistic upset path exists.

Clemson lost Welling (ACL) and Hunter (broken hand) in the ACC tournament, but their remaining core still has a higher KenPom rating than Iowa. Iowa enters cold at 4-6 in their last 10 with a first-year coach adjusting from D2.

McNeese is a classic 12-seed upset threat riding a 10-0 streak, but Vanderbilt beat Florida and Tennessee in the SEC tournament proving they compete against elite talent. Vandy's ball security neutralizes McNeese's turnover-forcing identity.

Nebraska's 22.4 AdjEM and former 24-game win streak show sustained excellence. Troy has a negative efficiency margin (-1.9) and a negative turnover margin from the Sun Belt. The talent gap is too wide.

UNC lost star Caleb Wilson (broken thumb, projected top-5 pick) and went 5-5 in their last 10 -- flagged as cold. VCU is 9-1 in their last 10 with a HAVOC pressing defense that forces turnovers at a 21.4% rate. UNC's -1.3 turnover margin makes them vulnerable to exactly this style of chaos.

Illinois has Big Ten pedigree and an 18.5 AdjEM. Penn is an Ivy League team at -1.8 AdjEM that squeaked through their conference tournament in OT with a 3-7 close-game record. The athleticism gap is enormous.

Saint Mary's controls tempo brilliantly (63.2 pace), but Texas A&M's SEC-tested physicality and significantly higher SOS (85.6 vs 64.8) gives them the edge. Texas A&M has faced the kind of athletes that Saint Mary's deliberate approach rarely encounters.

Houston has the nation's best defense (89.8 AdjD), the best turnover margin in the field (+5.6), and Kelvin Sampson's 20 tournament appearances. Idaho is making their first tournament since 1990 with a -6.2 AdjEM. A 25+ point win.

Duke is the nation's highest-rated team by AdjEM (32.9) with a 32-2 record and 11-game win streak. Siena is a MAAC champion with a -9.2 AdjEM. No 16-seed has beaten a 1-seed since 2018 and Duke isn't that kind of vulnerable.

TCU is 8-2 in their last 10 with Big 12 toughness under Jamie Dixon (16 career tournament appearances). Ohio State has a first-time tournament HC, key injuries (Noel out since January, Chatman day-to-day), and a .500 close-game record. TCU's experience tips this.

St. John's is massively underseeded at KenPom 6 with a 31-5 record, Big East championship, and Rick Pitino's 55-22 career tournament record. Northern Iowa's weak SOS (52.4) means they haven't faced this caliber of guard play all season.

Bill Self's tournament machine (57-24 career record) handles mid-majors efficiently. Cal Baptist is making their first D1 tournament ever with a 2.2 AdjEM from the WAC. The experience and talent gap compounds under March pressure.

South Florida is riding a 9-game win streak and emotional momentum from coach Amir Abdur-Rahim's passing, but their SOS (62.4) is far below Louisville's ACC gauntlet. Louisville's physicality and talent edge barely win out despite Kelsey's 0-6 career tournament record.

Tom Izzo in March: 59-26 tournament record, 8 Final Fours, 28 straight appearances. This senior-led team with 11 tournament-experienced players will impose their defensive will on NDSU's Summit League offense.

Both teams have significant injuries. UCLA's leading scorer Bilodeau and guard Dent are limited; UCF's Stillwell and Bol are banged up. UCLA's Big Ten battle-testing and Cronin's tournament pedigree (16-15 career, Final Four) narrowly tip this over UCF's Big 12 experience.

Dan Hurley seeking a three-peat with the most tournament-experienced roster in the field (10 players with March experience, 15-5 career record). Furman's -4.4 AdjEM and 2-7 close-game record offer no upset path against this caliber of program.
Arizona is 32-2, Big 12 champions, the healthiest 1-seed, and running hot at 9-1 in their last 10. LIU is a NEC auto-bid. Arizona's 30.0 AdjEM and elite two-way efficiency make this over by halftime.

A true coin flip. Villanova's Big East DNA and Jay Wright-era institutional knowledge still permeates the program. Utah State's MWC schedule provides competitive experience, but Villanova's March mystique and coaching pedigree tip the narrowest of margins.

Wisconsin's grind-it-out style is a Cinderella killer. They'll reduce this to 55 possessions where High Point's Big South-level athletes can't find clean looks against Wisconsin's half-court defense. The Badgers are built to survive and advance.

John Calipari brings 8 Final Four appearances and SEC-caliber athleticism to this matchup. Hawaii faces the longest travel distance in the tournament (4,000+ miles) and a massive step up in competition. Arkansas's defensive pressure overwhelms.

Texas is the most dangerous 11-seed in the field -- raw talent from the Big 12 that knows BYU's tendencies from conference play. BYU's defensive scheme is vulnerable to Texas's size inside. The familiarity factor and higher ceiling give Texas the slight edge.

Gonzaga's elite offensive firepower under Mark Few will carve apart Kennesaw State's defense. Kennesaw State's point-shaving suspension drama adds chaos, but their Conference USA pedigree doesn't translate to competing with Gonzaga's offensive efficiency.

Missouri's SEC physicality and higher SOS give them the edge in a matchup of similarly-rated teams. Miami's ACC struggles down the stretch and lower conference strength score make them vulnerable. Missouri's defensive versatility creates problems Miami's offense hasn't consistently solved.

Purdue's Big Ten size advantage and 22.4 AdjEM against Queens' first D1 tournament appearance is a 30-point mismatch. The interior dominance alone -- Purdue's frontcourt vs Queens' undersized lineup -- makes this a non-competitive game.

Michigan is a 1-seed with a 26.4 AdjEM and Dusty May's Final Four pedigree from FAU. UMBC made 16-over-1 history in 2018 but this roster is a -12.0 AdjEM with a 2-8 close-game record. Lightning doesn't strike the same program twice.

Saint Louis is 28-5 with system basketball under Josh Schertz and 8-2 in their last 10. Georgia had an early SEC tournament exit and flat 6-4 last-10 stretch. Saint Louis's disciplined execution and better recent trajectory tip this 8-9 game.

Texas Tech's defense is elite (92.1 AdjD) with the lowest turnover rate in the field (14.4%). Their defensive DNA under McCasland and Big 12 toughness will smother Akron's MAC-caliber offense. The 12-seed upset threat is real but Texas Tech's floor is too high.

Alabama's three-point volume (44% rate at 37.8% accuracy) will overwhelm Hofstra's CAA-level defense. Nate Oats's tournament experience (11-6 record) and SEC talent advantage create an impossible task for a 13-seed.

Tennessee's defense-first identity under Rick Barnes (21 tournament appearances, 28-26 record) is built for March. SMU's best defender B.J. Edwards missed 5 games with an ankle injury clouding his availability. Tennessee's +3.4 turnover margin and SEC physicality prevail.

Virginia's post-Bennett system under Sanchez produced a 22.6 AdjEM and ACC runner-up finish. Wright State has a -2.4 AdjEM and 3-7 close-game record from the Horizon League. Virginia's half-court execution in a slow-tempo game is a death sentence for mid-majors.

Kentucky is devastated by injuries: Quaintance out for opening weekend, Lowe out for season, Moreno questionable. A freshman-heavy roster (avg 1.8 years) without its best players against Santa Clara's healthy, experienced squad (beat Saint Mary's in WCC semis) is a recipe for the 10-over-7 upset.

Iowa State has the highest SOS in the entire field (88.1), a 25.5 AdjEM, and Otzelberger's best squad. Tennessee State from the OVC has a -7.0 AdjEM and 2-7 close-game record. Complete mismatch in every measurable dimension.

round_of_32.reasoning: Florida's defending champion pedigree and nation-best AdjEM overwhelm an injury-depleted Clemson. Without Welling and Hunter, Clemson lacks the scoring punch to keep pace with Florida's balanced frontcourt trio of Haugh, Condon, and Chinyelu.

Nebraska's 22.4 AdjEM and Big Ten physicality edge Vanderbilt's 16.6 AdjEM. Hoiberg's squad had a 24-game win streak proving they sustain excellence over long stretches. The size and depth advantage wears Vanderbilt down.

VCU's Cinderella energy from the UNC upset won't carry against Illinois's Big Ten talent. Illinois handles pressure better than UNC does, and their pace-and-space offense creates clean looks that VCU's press can't prevent. VCU's 68.2% FT shooting will cost them in crunch time.

Houston's nation-best defense suffocates Texas A&M's middling offense. Sampson's squad has the experience, the +5.6 turnover margin, and the defensive identity to dominate this in-state matchup. Houston allows the fewest points per half-court possession nationally.

Duke's 32.9 AdjEM is the second-highest in the field. Even without Caleb Foster, Duke's freshman talent pool is deeper than TCU's entire rotation. Scheyer's ACC championship run and 11-game win streak show a team peaking in March.

St. John's is the most underseeded team in the bracket (KenPom 6, 31-5, Big East champs). Kansas is 5-5 in their last 10 with lingering effects from Peterson's missed games. Pitino's 55-22 tournament record and St. John's guard-driven attack carve up a slumping Kansas defense.

Tom Izzo in the second round is historically lethal. Michigan State's senior-led roster (11 tournament-experienced players, avg 3.2 years) will outmuscle Louisville's freshman-heavy squad. Kelsey's 0-6 career tournament record is a red flag against Izzo's 59-26.

UConn's championship DNA under Hurley (15-5 tournament record, back-to-back titles) and roster with 10 tournament-experienced players overwhelm an injury-riddled UCLA. The three-peat bid stays alive with depth and institutional March knowledge.

Arizona is the healthiest and most complete 1-seed with a 30.0 AdjEM and Big 12 championship momentum. Villanova's March pedigree is real but their current talent level is a tier below Arizona's two-way dominance. Arizona's offensive efficiency (121.4 AdjO) creates separation.

Calipari's SEC athleticism and up-tempo push forces Wisconsin out of their grinding comfort zone. Arkansas wants 70+ possessions; Wisconsin wants 58. When forced to play faster than they prefer, Wisconsin's efficiency drops significantly. Calipari's 8 Final Four appearances reflect a coach who knows how to leverage athletic advantages.

Gonzaga's offensive efficiency under Mark Few is difficult to game-plan against in a single-elimination setting. Texas's upset energy from the BYU win doesn't sustain against Gonzaga's systematic shooting -- the Zags generate open looks against any defensive scheme at a rate that overwhelms talent-based upsets.

Purdue's size advantage and elite offensive efficiency (118.6 AdjO) will overwhelm Missouri's interior. The rebounding differential projects at +10 or more for Purdue. Missouri's defensive versatility won't matter when conceding that many second-chance points.

Michigan's 1-seed talent and 26.4 AdjEM dwarfs Saint Louis's A-10 pedigree. Schertz's system play is effective against mid-major competition but Michigan's Big Ten depth and guard play operate at a different speed. The Wolverines pull away in the second half.

The ultimate style clash: Texas Tech's elite defense (92.1 AdjD) vs Alabama's three-point-heavy offense (44% 3PA rate). When Alabama's threes aren't falling, they have no Plan B -- and Texas Tech's perimeter defense is designed to take away exactly those shots. Tech's +2.4 turnover margin controls the game.

Two elite defensive teams in a game that will be played in the 50s. Tennessee's SEC-tested physicality, superior forced turnover rate (18.8% vs 17.0%), and Rick Barnes's 21 tournament appearances give them the edge. Barnes knows how to prepare for Virginia's style.

Iowa State's defensive pressure and nation-best SOS are a bridge too far for Santa Clara's Cinderella run. The Cyclones' ability to force turnovers against WCC-caliber ball-handling will create 8-10 extra possessions. Iowa State's Big 12 toughness ends the fairy tale.

sweet_16.reasoning: Florida's defending champion experience and superior two-way efficiency make them the pick. Nebraska's 24-game win streak was impressive but their Big Ten quarterfinal exit showed vulnerability when tested. Florida's frontcourt trio dominates the paint.

Houston's defense is the great equalizer -- they hold opponents below 0.90 PPP in half-court settings. Illinois blew a double-digit lead in the Big Ten tournament, revealing composure issues Houston's suffocating defense will ruthlessly exploit. Sampson's 4 Final Four appearances as HC are the resume.

The marquee Sweet 16 matchup: Scheyer's freshmen vs Pitino's guards. Duke's 32.9 AdjEM and defensive versatility narrowly edge St. John's higher-variance perimeter attack. St. John's 0.034 three-point variance risk makes them boom-or-bust, and Duke's defense tips the odds toward bust.

A coaching masterclass: Izzo's grit vs Hurley's championship system. UConn's three-peat chase and deeper talent pool narrowly edges Michigan State's senior experience. UConn scores efficiently from all three levels, making them nearly adjustment-proof even against Izzo's game-planning.

Arizona's 30.0 AdjEM and Big 12 title form will overpower Arkansas. Arizona is the healthiest top seed and matches Arkansas's tempo while being more efficient on both ends. Calipari's first year at Arkansas limits his system's full maturity in a matchup requiring precision.

Purdue's Big Ten physicality and interior presence neutralize Gonzaga's offensive flow. Gonzaga's weaker SOS (WCC-heavy schedule) means they haven't faced this level of interior defense consistently. Purdue's post dominance and rebounding create the margin in a grind game.

Michigan's deep guard corps and offensive system create enough ball movement to crack even Texas Tech's elite defense. Michigan's 26.4 AdjEM vs Tech's 23.8 and Michigan's superior close-game record (7-2 vs 5-4) give them the edge when the defensive intensity tightens the game.

Iowa State's offensive efficiency (119.4 AdjO) and elite three-point shooting (37.0% on 40% rate) break through Tennessee's physical defense. Both teams are elite defensively, but Iowa State's shooting creates separation that Tennessee's half-court offense can't match.

elite_8.reasoning: The South Regional is at Toyota Center in Houston -- a de facto home game for the Cougars. Houston's nation-best defense will contain Florida's balanced attack, and the crowd factor cannot be overstated. Sampson's experienced roster (+72% returning minutes) stays composed in the biggest moments.

Duke's 32.9 AdjEM is the clear best of any remaining team. The Foster injury concern is real, but Duke's freshman depth provides answers at every position. This Duke squad has the highest ceiling in the tournament, and their 11-game win streak entering March shows they're peaking at the right time.

Arizona is the safest championship-caliber team remaining: healthiest, most balanced, Big 12 champions, and playing with supreme confidence. Purdue's size advantage is real but Arizona's perimeter shooting and defensive versatility control the style matchup. Arizona doesn't need to play inside to win.

Iowa State's nation-best SOS means they've been pressure-tested against the best teams in the country all season long. Michigan's guard-heavy approach is effective but Iowa State's Big 12 defensive intensity and Otzelberger's system designed for March grinds will contain Michigan's perimeter attack. Iowa State's 7-2 close-game record proves they finish in tight ones.

final_four.reasoning: Houston's defense is the best unit on either side and Sampson's 4 Final Four appearances provide invaluable stage experience. Duke's freshmen have never played in this environment. Houston grinds this into a 55-50 rock fight that favors the more experienced, more disciplined team with the best defense remaining.

Arizona's complete, healthy roster edges Iowa State in a matchup between two elite two-way teams separated by 4.5 AdjEM. Arizona's Big 12 championship included a head-to-head win over Houston, proving they handle elite defensive pressure. Lloyd's 6-2 tournament coaching record vs Otzelberger's 9-5 both show March competence, but Arizona's offensive ceiling is higher.

championship.reasoning: Houston wins the national championship in a defensive masterpiece. Kelvin Sampson's defense holds Arizona to their lowest offensive output of the season, and Houston's experienced roster (72% returning minutes) stays composed in crunch time. The Cougars' +5.6 turnover margin -- best in the field -- forces Arizona into uncharacteristic mistakes. After four Final Four appearances under Sampson, Houston finally cuts down the nets.
